ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Asian-style almond cookie; A0CJL (Biscuits and cookies) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one cookie weighing approximately 20 g, commonly consumed as a snack or festive treat.

nutrient_source.title: Almond Cookie (Asian Style) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Almond Cookie (Asian Style), CR ID F11080001, with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Cookies and Biscuits — Analogous estimate based on shortbread/butter cookie GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Asian-style almond cookie found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: butter/shortbread-style cookies (GI range 55–64) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 80.72 × 0.20) / 100 = 8.9 per 20 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 807 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid Asian-style almond cookies; a single 20 g cookie delivers approximately 161 mg sodium, and regular consumption can rapidly erode the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, worsening blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (38.09 g/100 g) and high carbohydrate (80.72 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Asian-style almond cookies; the combination of high sugar, high refined carbohydrate, and zero dietary fibre causes rapid blood glucose spikes, and even a single 20 g cookie contributes approximately 16 g of carbohydrate with no fibre buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (807 mg/100 g), elevated saturated fat (~5.5 g/100 g), and high sugar (38.09 g/100 g) collectively raise cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Asian-style almond cookies; the high sodium content elevates blood pressure, saturated fat contributes to LDL cholesterol elevation, and high sugar intake promotes triglyceride accumulation, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asian-style almond cookie (杏仁饼, Xìngrén bǐng) is a traditional Chinese butter cookie made from wheat flour, ground almonds, butter, sugar, and almond extract, baked until golden and crisp. It is a staple festive snack in Chinese communities across Singapore, Malaysia, and China, particularly popular during Chinese New Year celebrations. The cookie has a firm, crumbly texture with a rich buttery flavour and a pronounced almond aroma from both ground almonds and almond extract. Per 100 g, it delivers 474 kcal, 80.72 g carbohydrate, 38.09 g sugar, 14.79 g fat, 4.48 g protein, and a notably high sodium content of 807 mg. Dietary fibre is absent at 0 g per 100 g. The high sodium level is clinically significant and far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern. The high sugar and refined carbohydrate content with zero fibre makes this food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous butter cookie data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 8.9 per 20 g serving. Calcium is present at 135 mg/100 g, likely contributed by dairy butter and almond content. This food is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten, tree nuts, eggs, and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: Is almond cookie high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080001) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable butter cookies and documented in notes.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is a refined biscuit product with no whole-grain or high-fibre ingredients. iron_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source for this product formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Asian-style almond cookie are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 5.5 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for butter cookies (USDA NDB 18158) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is an adaptation and is flagged accordingly. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on shortbread/butter cookie data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Asian-style almond cookie was found. The input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' as GI source with confidence 0.333; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate per approved source policy. The health_suitability entries from the input for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100 g); trans_fat_g is set to -1 as this value was not confirmed by an approved source and should not be treated as a confirmed nutrient value.
